text:
Since 2012, the European Roma Right Centre (ERRC) has been monitoring incidents of targeted violence involving deaths and serious injuries against Roma. This violence, combined with ensuing fear and enforced displacement, has forced Roma to seek asylum in other EU countries, including from their original countries of residence in the Czech Republic, Hungary and Italy. The basis of current EU asylum policy, however, is that all member states are considered to be “safe countries of origin” and that another EU country should not provide asylum to a citizen of another EU country, except in the most exceptional circumstances. As a result, coupled with the fact that the residency requirements that Roma are generally unable to fulfil, Roma are left with few legal options and are often forced into a state of unlawful residence.

A parallel humanitarian crisis also exists for approximately 100,000 Roma who fled Kosovo a decade ago fleeing large scale violence. Many were forced to flee their new homes in Western Europe and have been ordered for removal to Kosovo. Once again, they will be exposed to discriminatory practices, physical intimidation and great socio-economic deprivation. The very limited opportunities for successful re-settlement, are underscored by the fact that some 70-75 % of returnees immediately fled Kosovo, after being there only a short period of time. A highly daunting experience for children who, having either been born or grown up entirely in the country of deportation, do not know the country they are being sent to.

The EU's system for failing to protect the fundamental rights of asylum seekers needs to be addressed, in particular by providing individual and proper assessments of their applications, so that these applicants can sufficiently contest the ‘presumption of safety’ within the EU, the rapporteur said. She also urged an immediate re-examination of the readmission of Roma to Kosovo. She called on member states to suspend readmissions and to instead consider alternative solutions such as integrating Roma in the countries where they are currently living. Returns should only be carried out in cases where this is necessary, and with proper protection and support measures to guarantee the safeguarding of their dignity and security.

prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
